title: What you are buying when you buy a stock
subtitle: A share of stock is not a ticker symbol or a price chart. It is a legal claim on the future cash flows of a real business. Start there.

body.content: Every share of common stock represents a proportional ownership interest in a company. That ownership confers: a claim on residual earnings after all other obligations are met, voting rights on major corporate decisions, and a claim on assets in a liquidation scenario (junior to debt holders). This is not abstract — it is a legal relationship with specific rights and risks.

body.label: The equity claim
body.content: Equity holders are residual claimants. They get paid last — after employees, suppliers, debt holders, and tax authorities. In exchange for this subordinated position, they receive unlimited upside participation. If the business grows significantly, the value of their claim grows proportionally. No cap.

What makes equity valuable is the present value of all future free cash flows generated by the business, discounted at a rate that reflects the risk of those cash flows. This is not a metaphor — it is the mathematical definition. A stock's intrinsic value is the sum of all the money the business will ever generate for shareholders, discounted to today.
Why the market price differs from intrinsic value

The market price at any given moment reflects the aggregate expectations, risk appetite, and liquidity needs of millions of buyers and sellers. These expectations are often wrong — sometimes dramatically so. This is both the challenge and the opportunity in equity investing: the gap between price and value is where returns are made.

When you buy a stock, you are buying future earnings. The price is what others currently believe those earnings are worth. The question is whether that belief is correct — and whether you have reason to think differently.
